title: 第 12 節
en: ' M. Lang m'a fait l'honneur de me citer,' he writes, ' comme un de ses allies, et j'ai lieu de croire que M. Gaidoz en fait en quelque mesure autant. Ces messieurs n'ont point entierement tort. Cependant je dois m'elever, au nom de la science mythologique et de l'exactitude dont elle ne peut pas plus se passer que les autres sciences, contre une methode qui ne fait que glisser sur des problemes de premiere importance,' &c.
Speaking of the whole method followed by those who actually claimed to have founded a new school of mythology, he says (p. 21) : —

' Je crains toutefois que ce qui s'y trouve de vrai ne soit connu depuis longtemps, et que la nouvelle ecole ne peche par exclusionisme tout autant que les ainees quelle combat avec tant de conviction.'

This is exactly what I have always said. What is there new in comparing the customs and myths of the Greeks with those of the barbarians ? Has not even Plato done this. Did anybody doubt that the Greeks, nay even the Hindus, were uncivilised or savages, before they became civilised or tamed ? Was not this common sense view, so strongly insisted on by Fontenelle and Vico in the eighteenth century, carried even to excess by such men as De Brosses (1709-1 771)? And have the lessons taught to De Brosses by his witty contemporaries been quite forgotten? Must his followers be told again and again that they ought to begin with a critical examination of the evidence put before them by casual travellers, and that mythology is as little made up of one and the same material as the crust of the earth of granite only?
D 2 36 KRONOS AND POLYNESIAN FOLKLORE. [chap.
Kronos and Polynesian Folklore.

After many conversations with the Rev. W.W. Gill, I had ventured to explain one part of the myth of Kronos by a reference to Polynesian and Melanesian folklore, long before the new school triumphantly proclaimed that discovery as peculiarly its own. Prof. Tiele states that Preller already, in his Greek Mythology, instituted the same comparison, and shows at the same time that Mr. Lang's application of it is really faulty (see pp. 17, 27). I do not wish to claim any priority, and as I do not read all the Folklore Journals, the discovery, for all I know, may have been made long before my time. I only mention it here in order to show, as Prof. Tiele has done, that my own method of Comparative Mythology, call it etymological, genealogical, or anything else, does not exclude sound ethnological evidence from whatever quarter it may come. Why should it, if only it is vouched for by a real Polynesian or Melanesian scholar, as, for instance, by the Rev. W.W. Gill or Dr. Codrington? As soon as we know that Ina or Sina, in Mangaia the beloved of Tuna of the cocoanut, means the moon, her legends become transparent. Whether we should gain much by comparing her name with that of the Babylonian moon-goddess Sin, I doubt, but I gladly leave it to ethnologists to decide that question. As soon as it has been proved that Taramahetonga means south-wind, or Taramaakiaki sea-weed, we see a physical background, however distant, for the stories told of them.

Professor Tiele and I differ on several points, but we perfectly understand each other, and when we i] KR0N0S AND POLYNESIAN FOLKLORE. 37 have made a mistake, we readily confess it and correct it. But the railleries of M. Gaidoz are quite beyond my obtuse understanding, whereas what Prof. Tiele calls the 'juvenile impetuosity' of these students of customs and myths seems to me deficient even in that French raillerie which is truly called ' un jeu d'esprit de ceux qui n'en ont pas ! '

M. Gaidoz. M. Gaidoz might do such excellent work, particularly as a Celtic scholar, that it seems a pity he should not help us in digging on Aryan ground, where so little has as yet been done for Celtic customs and myths. He is far too much of a scholar to fall under the condemnation of Professor Tiele when he writes (p. 1 1) : —

' Ces braves gens qui, pour peu qu'ils aient lu un ou deux livres de mythologie et d'anthropologie et un ou deux recits de voyages, ne manqueront pas de se mettre a comparer a tort et a travers, et pour tout resultat produiront la confusion.'

This is strong language, but is it too strong % This confusion is to a great extent the result, I shall not say of ignorance, but of ignoring what has been written by special scholars, and particularly by students of Sanskrit.
It seems that nothing has aroused such opposition and such monotonous raillerie as our constant appeal to language and etymology as solvents of mythology.

The Influence of Language on Mythology. Whereas I have laboured hard all my life to show the inevitable influence of language on thought, I am told, once for all, that language had nothing 38 INFLUENCE OF LANGUAGE ON MYTHOLOGY, [chap.
to do with the origin of myths, or, if anything, 'not more than five per cent.' As an admission of indebtedness even five per cent, in these days is welcome.

I had published a large book on the ' Science of Thought,' and a large book may be called a large mistake. I knew few people would read it, but I felt bound all the same to explain, once for all, what I meant by the influence of language on thought, and in what sense I had called, and still call, mythology a disease of language and thought. I imagined I had made it clear that identity of language and thought could only be meant for inseparableness of thought and language. In the strict sense of the word, it is clear that no two things can ever be identical in this world. But I thought I had proved that language and thought are manifestations of one and the same energy. Even Mr. Darwin admitted in the end that signs are indispensable for the formation of abstract ideas, and what signs are more natural and more generally accepted than words ? Other biologists went even further, and Virchow admitted ' that only, after their perceptions have become fixed by language, are the senses brought to a conscious possession and a real understanding of them.' If, then, the ordinary signs of abstract ideas are words, and if, as Comparative Philology has proved, every appellative (with the exception of onomatopoeic words) presupposes an abstract idea embodied in a root, it would require but little consideration to understand that in the very first attempts at real language, the sign may react on what is signified. This action and reaction between the sign and what is signified, or, in other i] INFLUENCE OF LANGUAGE ON MYTHOLOGY. 39 words, between language and thought is, to put it in the simplest form, what is meant by an affection, a pathological affection, or, from another point of view, a disease of language. Anyhow, people who can bravely maintain that language has nothing to do with language and myth, would find it difficult to explain why in Greek /xv0o<; came to mean both word and myth.

If it is asked how, if language and thought are inseparable, they can react on one another, we must remember that language, which is originally an energy or action, becomes, by pronunciation, an act, i.e. something done, a product, which remains, independent of us, after the action is over. The word, as we hear it and learn it and repeat it, is no longer our creative act, but something apart from us, something past, which, however, like many things that are past, determines in many ways what is present in thought and speech.
How Gender influences Mythology.

Let me give one illustration. If the inherited portion of language did not react on thought, how should we explain so simple a case, not, however, without importance in the formation of mythology, as the reaction produced by the masculine and feminine terminations of nouns on the character signified by a word ?

As soon as we call the sun Suryas, it assumes a masculine, an active character, as brightening, enlivening, fertilising the world ; call it Surya, fern., or *Svara = Here, and we have before us a kind and beautiful woman, a bride, a wife, a mother, as the case may be.
The Moon.

It is well known that in the Teutonic languages the moon was originally masculine, as the ruler of times and seasons, and as the guardian of all the institutions dependent on times and seasons, and this at a period in the history of civilisation long before the course of the sun had been sufficiently watched to serve the same purpose.
We have Gothic mena, m., 0. H. G. mano, m., A. S. mona, m., Icel. mani, m., Greek fxrjv, m., Sk. mas, m. The names for month are the same, or slightly modified, but always masculine.
The Sun.

The sun, on the contrary, is Goth, sun no, fern., A. S. sunne, fern., and this can only have been due to the introduction of solar by the side of lunar chronometry. In Gothic we find not only sunno, fern., but likewise sunn a, masc. The Old Norse sol, however, is fern, only, and therefore not borrowed from Latin sol. As soon as mythology says anything about sun and moon, it is clear how it must submit to the fetters of language. If the Edda speaks of sun and moon as the children of Mundilfari, the giant who is supposed to make the heavens turn round, Mani, the moon, becomes at once his son, S61, the sun, his daughter.

In the Slavonic dialects the sun is chiefly named and conceived as feminine, and if that is once done, the whole family of the sky had to be rearranged accordingly. Hence, in an early stage, the sun with the Slaves 1 was a cow, the moon a calf, the stars goats. At a later time the sun is a beautiful maid, 1 Krek, 1. c, p. 300.

i] THE SUN. 41 like the dawn, playing on the meadow of the sky. Her children are the stars, and one of them, Ivan, calls the moon his father, the sun his mother, the gloaming his sister, and the grey falcon (morningstar?) his brother. In other songs, however, all this is changed. The sun becomes the father, the moon his son ; nay, sun, moon, and rain are represented as three brothers. In one song the sun is the mother of the dawn, in another her daughter, and in a third the brother of the moon1, just as Helios is the brother of Selene.

Who can fail to see the germs of mythology in all this ? And yet we are told ex cathedra that language has nothing to do with myths which tell us of the fates of the supreme deities, such as sun, moon, sky, rain. If there had been no distinction of gender should we have had one set of stories of the sun as a woman, another of the sun as a man ? And why are sexless languages, as Bleek has shown, so poor in mythology, if these small differences between Sk. as and a, Gr. 09 and a, Lat. us and a, had had nothing to do with thought, nothing with mythology? Should we have had in the Veda the myth of SavitW giving his daughter Surya (sun) to Soma (moon), and among the Slaves the myth contained in the following verses ? —
The Moon leads home the Sun, It was in the first spring.
The Sun rose early, The Moon left her, He took a walk alone, Fell in love with the Morning-star, Then Perkana was angry, 1 Krek, 1. c, p. 315.
And cleft him with his sword. 'Why hast thou gone away? Walking alone by night? Flirting with the Morning-star?' Then his heart was sorrowful.
zh: > M. Lang m'a fait l'honneur de me citer，他寫道，「作為他的盟友之一，我有理由相信蓋多茲先生在某種程度上也如此做。這兩位先生並非全無道理。然而，我必須以神話學科學及其與其他科學同樣不可或缺的精確性之名，反對一種僅在首要問題上輕輕帶過的方法。」等等。
談到那些實際宣稱創立了神話學新學派者所遵循的整體方法時，他說道（第21頁）：——

『Je crains toutefois que ce qui s'y trouve de vrai ne soit connu depuis longtemps, et que la nouvelle école ne pèche par exclusionisme tout autant que les aînées qu'elle combat avec tant de conviction.』

這正是我一向所說的。將希臘人的習俗與神話和野蠻人的相比，又有什麼新意可言？難道連柏拉圖不也做過此事嗎？難道有人懷疑過希臘人——甚至印度人——在他們變得文明或被馴化之前，曾是未開化或野蠻的嗎？難道這種常識性的觀點——十八世紀時由豐特奈爾與維柯極力主張——不是被德布羅斯（De Brosses，1709-1771）這類人物推行得甚至過火了嗎？而德布羅斯從他那些機智的同代人那裡學到的教訓，難道已被全然遺忘了嗎？難道必須一再告誡他的追隨者：他們應當先對偶然旅人所提供的證據進行批判性檢驗，而且神話學的材料絕非像地殼僅由花崗巖構成那樣單一？
在我們轉向比較神話學的領域之前，必須先釐清一個根本的方法論問題。語言科學的進展已經清楚顯示，若無嚴格的語音對應律，任何神話比較都將淪為任意的聯想。正如我們在雅利安與閃語族傳統中所見，唯有透過詞源學的檢證，才能區分真正的歷史傳承與後起的文學借用。

在與 W.W. 吉爾牧師多次交談後，我曾大膽地藉助玻里尼西亞與美拉尼西亞的民間傳說，來解釋克羅諾斯神話的某一部分；這遠在所謂的新學派得意洋洋地宣稱該發現為其獨有之前。蒂勒教授指出，普雷勒在其《希臘神話學》中早已提出相同的比較，同時也表明蘭格先生對此的應用實有謬誤（參見第 17、27 頁）。我無意主張任何優先權，況且我並未閱讀所有的民俗學期刊，就我所知，這項發現或許早在我的時代之前便已有人提出。我在此提及此事，僅是為瞭如同蒂勒教授所做的那樣，表明我自己的比較神話學方法——無論稱之為詞源學的、系譜學的，或其他任何名稱——並不排斥來自任何方面的可靠民族學證據。只要這證據是由真正的玻里尼西亞或美拉尼西亞學者——例如 W.W. 吉爾牧師或科德林頓博士——所擔保，它為何要被排除呢？一旦我們知道，在曼蓋亞島，椰子之鰻魚圖納所鍾愛的伊娜或西娜，意指月亮，關於她的傳說便變得清晰可解。至於將她的名字與巴比倫月神辛相比較，是否能讓我們獲益良多，我持懷疑態度，但我樂於將這個問題留給民族學家去裁決。一旦證實塔拉瑪赫湯加意指南風，或塔拉瑪阿基亞基意指海藻，我們便能看見關於它們的故事背後，無論多麼遙遠，都有一個物質性的背景。
蒂勒教授與我在數點上意見相左，但我們彼此完全理解；一旦犯錯，我們也樂於承認並改正。然而，蓋多茲先生的嘲諷完全超出我遲鈍的理解力；反之，蒂勒教授所謂這些習俗與神話研究者的「少年氣盛」，在我看來甚至缺乏那種真正被稱為「無才者之機鋒遊戲」的法式嘲諷。
M. Gaidoz。M. Gaidoz 或許能做出如此出色的工作，尤其作為一名凱爾特學者，可惜他未能協助我們在雅利安領域進行挖掘——該領域對凱爾特習俗與神話的研究至今仍極其匱乏。他身為學者，遠不至於落入提勒教授所譴責的境地，後者寫道（第 11 頁）：——

『Ces braves gens qui, pour peu qu'ils aient lu un ou deux livres de mythologie et d'anthropologie et un ou deux récits de voyages, ne manqueront pas de se mettre à comparer à tort et à travers, et pour tout résultat produiront la confusion.』
這是強烈的措辭，但是否過於強烈？這種混淆在很大程度上是——我不說是無知的結果，而是忽視專家學者，特別是梵文研究者著作的結果。
似乎沒有什麼比我們不斷訴諸語言與詞源學作為神話學的解析工具，更激起如此強烈的反對與單調的嘲諷了。
我終生竭力證明語言對思想無可避免的影響，如今卻被告知，語言與神話學毫無關聯——這真是令人費解。
> 「……與神話的起源有關，或者，真要說的話，『不超過百分之五』。」即便只是承認百分之五的負債，在當今這個時代也是受歡迎的。

我曾出版過一部關於「思想科學」的鉅著，而一部鉅著或許可稱之為一個巨大的錯誤。我深知願意閱讀它的人寥寥無幾，但我仍覺得有義務，一勞永逸地解釋清楚，我所謂語言對思想的影響究竟意指為何，以及我過去稱呼、如今仍稱神話為語言與思想的「疾病」究竟是何種意義。我原以為自己已清楚表明：語言與思想的同一性，只能理解為思想與語言的不可分離性。就該詞的嚴格意義而言，世上顯然沒有任何兩件事物能夠完全同一。但我自認已證明，語言與思想乃是同一種能量的兩種展現。就連達爾文先生最終也承認，符號對於抽象觀唸的形成是不可或缺的；而又有什麼符號比詞語更自然、更為普遍接受呢？其他生物學家甚至走得更遠，菲爾紹便承認：「唯有在知覺經由語言固定下來之後，感官才能對它們達到有意識的掌握與真正的理解。」那麼，如果抽象觀唸的普通符號就是詞語，並且，正如比較語言學所證明的，每一個通名（除了擬聲詞之外）都預設了一個體現在詞根中的抽象觀念，那麼只需稍加思考便能理解：在真正語言的初次嘗試中，符號就可能反過來作用於其所指之物。這種符號與所指之物之間的作用與反作用，或者換句話說，語言與思想之間的作用與反作用，最簡單地說，就是所謂的「感染」（affection）、一種病理性的感染，或者從另一個角度來看，就是語言的「疾病」。無論如何，那些能夠勇敢地主張語言與神話毫無關係的人，恐怕很難解釋，為什麼在希臘文中，μῦθος 這個詞會同時意指「詞語」與「神話」。

若有人問：既然語言與思想不可分割，它們又如何能相互作用？我們必須記得，語言原本是一種能量或行動（energy or action），經由發音而成為一種行為（act），亦即某種已完成之事、一種產物；當行為結束後，此產物仍獨立於我們而留存。我們所聽見、學習並重複的詞語，不再是我們創造性的行動，而是某種脫離我們自身之物、某種過去之物——然而，正如許多過去的事物一樣，它在諸多方面決定了思想與言說中的當下狀態。

在我們轉向比較神話學的具體問題之前，必須先處理一個更為根本的難題——即語言本身所蘊含的性別區分，如何深刻地形塑了神話的起源與發展。語言科學（the science of language）早已揭示，雅利安（Aryan）與閃語族（Semitic）的原始語言中，名詞被賦予了陽性、陰性與中性。這種語法性別並非任意標記，而是反映了早期人類將一切事物視為具有生命與性別的傾向。當人們稱呼天為「父」（Dyaus pitar，希臘文Ζεὺς πατήρ，拉丁文Jupiter），稱呼地為「母」（Prithivī mātā），他們並非在修辭，而是在表達一種原始的直觀：自然力量如同人類社會一般，存在著性別關係。 正是這種語言的本能，為神話提供了最初的素材。例如，在吠陀（Veda）中，天父特尤斯（Dyaus）與地母被視為宇宙的父母；而太陽、月亮、黎明等現象，則根據其語法性別被賦予男性或女性的神格。梨俱吠陀（Rig-Veda）中的黎明女神烏莎斯（Ushas）是陰性，而太陽神蘇利耶（Sūrya）是陽性——這並非基於對自然現象的客觀觀察，而是因為「黎明」一詞在梵文中屬陰性，「太陽」屬陽性。穆勒（Müller）因此指出，神話學的許多謎團，其實是「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）所造成：人們忘記了這些名詞原本只是比喻，反而將語法性別當作真實的性別，從而衍生出無數關於神祇婚姻、鬥爭與世系的故事。 這種現象在單一神教（henotheism）與輪換主神教（kathenotheism）的階段尤為明顯。吠陀詩人對不同神祇的讚頌，往往根據該神祇的語法性別與功能，賦予其相應的性別特質。例如，火神阿耆尼（Agni，陽性）被視為男性使者，而語言女神伐屍（Vāc，陰性）則被視為女性智慧之源。當這些神祇在後來的多神教（polytheism）體系中被固定下來時，性別便成為神話敘事中不可或缺的結構要素。 因此，要理解比較神話學（comparative mythology），就必須先認識語言科學（the science of language）所揭示的性別機制。神話並非純粹的幻想，而是人類在試圖表達對無限者（the Infinite）的敬畏時，不自覺地受到語言結構制約的產物。
讓我舉一個例子說明。如果語言的遺傳部分不對思想產生反作用，我們要如何解釋像名詞的陽性與陰性詞尾對詞語所表示的特性產生反作用這類簡單——但在神話形成中並非不重要——的情況呢？
一旦我們稱太陽為 Sūryas（陽性），它便呈現出陽性、主動的性格，即照亮、賦予生機、滋養世界；若稱之為 Sūryā（陰性），或 *Svarā（此處），我們眼前便出現一位和藹美麗的女性——視情況而定，可能是新娘、妻子或母親。

在宗教學的早期階段，月亮（Moon）無疑佔據著顯著的地位。它那週期性的盈虧、神秘的陰影，以及與夜晚和黑暗的聯繫，使其成為原始人類敬畏與崇拜的對象。不同於太陽那恆定不變的光芒，月亮的變化多端似乎暗示著一種更為隱晦、更難以捉摸的力量。許多神話體系中，月亮被視為掌管生育、潮汐、時間循環以及死亡與重生之神。例如，在印度吠陀（Veda）傳統中，月亮（Chandra 或 Soma）與一種神聖的飲料相關聯，被認為是眾神與祖先的飲品。而在希臘神話中，月亮女神塞勒涅（Selene）與阿耳忒彌斯（Artemis）則分別代表著月亮的純淨與狩獵的野性。這些不同的詮釋，反映了人類試圖理解並賦予這個天體以意義的普遍努力，也為比較神話學（comparative mythology）提供了豐富的素材。
眾所周知，在條頓語族語言中，月亮（moon）最初為陽性，因為它是時間與季節的統治者，也是所有依賴時間與季節之制度的守護者；而這發生在文明史的某個時期，遠早於太陽的運行被充分觀測以服務相同目的之前。
我們有哥德語 *mena*（陽性）、古高地德語 *mano*（陽性）、盎格魯-撒克遜語 *mona*（陽性）、冰島語 *mani*（陽性）、希臘語 μήν（陽性）、梵語 *mās*（陽性）。表示「月份」的名詞與此相同，或僅有細微變化，且始終為陽性。

太陽在雅利安宗教中佔有顯著地位，但絕非如某些學者所主張的那樣，是雅利安人崇拜的唯一對象。太陽本身被視為一位神祇，名為蘇利亞（Sūrya）、薩維塔（Savitṛ）、密多羅（Mitra）等。然而，太陽並非無限者（the Infinite）本身，而是無限者的一種顯現，一個象徵。當雅利安人說「太陽是神」時，他們並非意指那發光的圓盤本身即為神，而是指那在太陽之中、透過太陽運作、並使太陽成為可能的神秘力量。這種區別至關重要。太陽崇拜並非單純的拜物教；它是一種對自然力量的敬畏，這種力量被感知為某種超越其可見形式的存在。因此，太陽在雅利安宗教中扮演的角色，與其在閃語族（Semitic）宗教中扮演的角色截然不同。在閃語族宗教中，太陽往往被視為一位更直接、更具人格性的神祇，而在雅利安宗教中，太陽則更常被視為通往無限者（the Infinite）的一道門戶，一個引導心靈超越自身、趨向那不可見者的象徵。

反之，太陽在哥德語中為陰性名詞 *sunno*，古英語 *sunne* 亦為陰性；此現象唯有透過陽曆與太陰曆並行計時，引入太陽（solar）概念方能解釋。在哥德語中，我們不僅發現陰性的 *sunno*，亦有陽性的 *sunn a*。然而古諾斯語的 *sól* 僅為陰性，故非借自拉丁語 *sol*。一旦神話開始論及太陽與月亮，便清楚可見其如何受制於語言的桎梏。若《埃達》稱太陽與月亮為蒙迪爾法利（Mundilfari）——那位據說推動天穹運轉的巨人——之子嗣，則月亮（Mani）立時成為其子，太陽（Sól）則為其女。
在斯拉夫方言中，太陽主要被命名並構想為陰性；一旦如此，整個天空家族便須據此重新安排。因此，在早期階段，對斯拉夫人而言，太陽是一頭母牛，月亮是一隻牛犢，星辰則是山羊¹。到了後期，太陽成為一位美麗的少女¹。 ¹ Krek, 前引書，第300頁。
41 如黎明般，在天空的草原上嬉戲。她的孩子是星辰，其中一位名叫伊凡，稱月亮為父、太陽為母、暮光為姊妹、灰隼（晨星？）為兄弟。然而在其他歌謠中，這一切全然改變。太陽成為父親，月亮成為其子；不僅如此，太陽、月亮與雨水被描繪為三兄弟。在某首歌謠中，太陽是黎明的母親，在另一首中則是她的女兒，在第三首中又成了月亮的兄弟¹，正如赫利俄斯是塞勒涅的兄弟一般。

誰能看不見這一切之中神話的萌芽？然而，我們卻聽聞權威宣稱，語言與那些講述太陽、月亮、天空、雨水等至高神祇命運的神話毫無關聯。倘若沒有性別的區分，我們還會有一系列關於太陽為女性、太陽為男性的故事嗎？而正如布利克所指出，如果梵文的 *as* 與 *a*、希臘文的 *09* 與 *a*、拉丁文的 *us* 與 *a* 這些微小差異，與思想無關、與神話無關，那麼為何無性別的語言在神話上如此貧乏？我們是否會在《吠陀》中看到薩維特里將女兒蘇利亞（太陽）嫁給索摩（月亮）的神話，以及在斯拉夫人中看到下列詩句所蘊含的神話？——

**月亮引領太陽歸家，那是在最初的春天。** > 這句開場白引自《梨俱吠陀》（Rig-Veda）中的一首頌詩，它描繪了遠古雅利安人（Aryan）對天體運行的神話式想像。在穆勒看來，這類詩句正是宗教學（the science of religion）所要探究的原始材料——它們既非純粹的詩歌，亦非成熟的教義，而是人類心靈在面對「無限者」（the Infinite）時所留下的最初印記。 穆勒指出，若要理解宗教的起源，就必須先釐清語言與思維之間的關係。他反覆強調，神話與宗教中的許多荒謬之處，其實源於語言的誤用——所謂「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）。例如，當古人說「太陽追趕月亮」時，他們並非在進行天文觀測，而是將自然現象人格化，並在語法結構中不自覺地創造出神祇。這種現象在吠陀（Veda）文獻中尤為明顯：雅利安人稱蒼穹為「特尤斯」（Dyaus），稱光芒為「提婆」（Deva），這些詞彙原本只是描述自然現象的普通名詞，卻在語言的演變中逐漸被賦予神聖的位格。 穆勒進一步區分了宗教發展的幾個階段：首先是「單一神教」（henotheism）或「輪換主神教」（kathenotheism）——在這一階段，信徒並非崇拜多個神祇，而是在不同情境下輪流將某一位神祇視為至高無上的存在；隨後才逐漸演化為「多神教」（polytheism）與「一神教」（monotheism）。他特別強調，這種演化並非線性的進步，而是人類心靈在語言與神話的雙重製約下，不斷嘗試接近「無限者」的過程。 為了證明這一觀點，穆勒大量援引吠陀文獻，並將其與閃語族（Semitic）及圖蘭語族（Turanian）的宗教文本進行比較。他認為，唯有透過「比較神學」（comparative theology）與「比較神話學」（comparative mythology）的雙重方法，才能真正揭示宗教的本質。而這一切，都必須建立在「語言科學」（the science of language）的堅實基礎之上——因為語言不僅是思想的載體，更是神話與宗教的溫床。
太陽早早升起，月亮離她而去，他獨自散步，愛上了晨星，於是佩爾卡納（Perkana）發怒了¹。 ¹ 克雷克（Krek），前引書，第315頁。
他以劍將其劈開。「你為何離去？獨自夜行？與晨星調情？」隨後他心中充滿悲傷。
